The Mermaid block diagrams above substitute physical schematics with **functional architecture flows** that map regulatory and analytical interactions — this is the **KTU-recommended visualisation strategy** for pharmaceutical process topics. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Assessment Pattern Reference** > - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct short-answer, definition-based, recall-level. Time: ~6 min. > - **Part B (14 marks):** Choice-based, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Time: ~25 min. > - **Cognitive levels (Bloom's Revised):** L1 Remember, L2 Understand, L3 Apply, L4 Analyse. > - **CO Mapping:** Module 4 maps to **CO4** — *Evaluate quality and regulatory challenges in advanced biological products.* --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]** **Define the term "biosimilar" as per EMA/US-FDA guidelines. How is it fundamentally different from a generic drug?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A biosimilar is a biological medicinal product that is **highly similar to an authorised reference biological product** in terms of **quality, safety, and efficacy**, with no clinically meaningful differences, as established through a comprehensive comparability exercise. **(1.5 marks)** It differs from a generic drug because: (i) biologics are **large, complex molecules** produced in **living cells**, unlike small molecules synthesised chemically; (ii) generics require only **bioequivalence** proof, whereas biosimilars require **analytical + non-clinical + clinical** comparability; (iii) biosimilars show **inherent micro-heterogeneity** due to biological manufacturing, so "similarity" is a *demonstrated scientific conclusion* rather than absolute identity. **(1.5 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]** **What is the "Totality of Evidence" (ToE) principle in biosimilar approval?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Totality of Evidence is the integrated, multi-domain evidence principle governing biosimilar approval. **(1 mark)** It requires convergence of three sequential evidence streams — **(i) analytical similarity** (the cornerstone, covering primary structure, higher-order structure, PTMs, biological activity, purity/impurities), **(ii) non-clinical similarity** (in-vitro receptor binding, cell-based bioassays, and one relevant species animal study), and **(iii) clinical similarity** (PK/PD equivalence, comparative efficacy/safety trial, and immunogenicity assessment). **(1.5 marks)** The strength of the biosimilar claim emerges from the integrated weight of all three streams; failure in any one domain cannot be compensated by the others. **(0.5 marks)** --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choice-Based) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, May 2024]** **Q3. (a) Discuss in detail the regulatory framework for biosimilar approval in the EU, US, and India. Compare the pathways critically. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) European Union (EMA) Pathway — 7 marks** - The EMA issued the **first-ever biosimilar guideline in 2006**, coining the term "biosimilar." It later released overarching and product-specific guidelines. - The framework is governed by **Directive 2001/83/EC** and the **EMA Biosimilar Guideline (CHMP/437/04 Rev 1)**. - Reference product must be **EMA-authorised**; the development is **comparability-driven**, not full-stand-alone. - The **CHMP (Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use)** evaluates the dossier; **scientific advice** is recommended pre-submission. - Extrapolation is permitted with **scientific justification**. **(ii) United States (US-FDA) Pathway — 7 marks** - Governed by the **Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act (BPCIA), 2010**, codified in the **Public Health Service Act § 351(k)**. - Pathway is a **stand-alone, comparative biologics application** (not an ANDA). - Reference product must be **FDA-licensed**; if non-US licensed, a **scientific bridge** is mandatory. - **Interchangeability** designation (additional) allows pharmacy-level substitution — requires **switching study** data. - Distinctive: **Pink Sheet** designation, **Purple Book** listing. **(iii) India (CDSCO) Pathway — 7 marks** - Governed by the **"Guidelines on Similar Biologics" (2012, revised 2016)**, jointly developed by **CDSCO and DBT (Department of Biotechnology)**. - Reference product must be licensed in **India or a reference regulatory authority** (US, EU, UK, Canada, Australia, Japan). - **Stepwise regulatory process** — pre-submission consultation, review by **RCGM (Review Committee on Genetic Manipulation)** for pre-clinical data, and **CDSCO** for clinical and marketing approval. - **Phase III comparative clinical trial** in Indian population typically required. - Indian regulator follows the **"totality of evidence"** principle but emphasises **local clinical data** more strongly. > **Critical Comparison:** (Mention any 2 of these for 1 mark each) > - All three rely on comparability rather than full re-demonstration of efficacy. > - EMA = most experienced; US = interchangeability uniqueness; India = local clinical trial emphasis. > - All require reference product from SRA or local authority. --- **Q3. (b) Explain the principles of analytical similarity assessment in biosimilars. Discuss in detail the tier-based classification of quality attributes. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** Analytical similarity is the **cornerstone** of the biosimilar dossier, providing the foundation upon which all subsequent non-clinical and clinical studies rest. **(0.5 marks)** **Tier-based classification (per FDA Draft Guidance, 2017):** **Tier 1 — Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs):** These have the **highest impact on clinical outcomes** and include **biological activity, receptor binding, and certain CQAs (e.g., ADCC-relevant afucosylation, complement activation).** Acceptable range: within **$\pm k\sigma$ of the reference QR** (typically $k=1.5$–$3$). Statistical equivalence testing is most rigorous at this tier. **(2 marks)** **Tier 2 — Key Quality Attributes (KQAs):** Important for safety/efficacy but with less direct impact. Examples include **HMW aggregates, certain glycan species, oxidation hotspots.** Range: within **$\pm 3\sigma$ to $\pm 4\sigma$** of reference QR, or qualitative justification. **(1.5 marks)** **Tier 3 — Other Quality Attributes:** Have **minimal direct impact** on clinical outcomes. Examples include **minor process-related impurities, subvisible particles (within limits).** Range: **pharmacopoeial compliance** suffices, with monitoring. **(1 mark)** **Methods used across tiers:** - **Tier 1:** Cell-based bioassays, SPR, HDX-MS. - **Tier 2:** UPLC-MS glycan analysis, SEC-MALS, peptide mapping. - **Tier 3:** ELISA, residual DNA assays. **Statistical Approaches:** - **Equivalence test:** Mean within $\pm\Delta$ with 90 % CI approach. - **Quality range (QR) approach:** Biosimilar values fall within reference product's variability range. - **Tolerability interval:** Established from reference product data; biosimilar must fall within. **Reporting:** Data presented as **equivalence plot, side-by-side overlaid chromatograms, fingerprint analysis, multivariate data analysis (e.g., PCA, MVA).** **(1 mark)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Exam Pattern, Dec 2023]** **Q4. (a) What are the major manufacturing challenges encountered during biosimilar development? Discuss the critical process parameters (CPPs) and their impact on critical quality attributes (CQAs). (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Manufacturing Challenges (3.5 marks):** - **Cell line stability** — risk of genetic drift in long-term culture, affecting product consistency. - **Process scalability** — moving from lab to commercial scale can shift glycoform profiles. - **Process consistency** — every fermentation run is inherently variable; lot-to-lot reproducibility is the chief QC challenge. - **Analytical method transfer** — methods qualified at innovator scale must be re-validated for biosimilar maker. - **Purity profile** — meeting innovator's aggregate, fragment, and impurity specifications without exact process knowledge. - **Intellectual property** — navigating process patents held by innovator (e.g., cell-line, media composition, purification matrices). - **Comparability** — maintaining the biosimilar product's similarity *to itself* as process improvements occur post-approval. **(ii) CPPs and CQAs (3.5 marks):** | Critical Process Parameter (CPP) | Impact on CQA | |---|---| | Temperature ($T$) | Protein folding, aggregation, oxidation | | pH | Charge variants, deamidation, isomerisation | | Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | Glycosylation pattern (especially for mAbs) | | Glucose / feed strategy | Glycan occupancy, productivity | | Harvest day | HMW aggregate content, proteolysis | | Purification chromatography | HCP, residual DNA, leached Protein A | | Formulation pH & excipients | Stability, aggregation on storage | | Lyophilisation cycle (if applicable) | Cake appearance, reconstitution, potency | **The CQA ↔ CPP relationship is modelled via DoE (Design of Experiments) and monitored through the QbD (Quality by Design) framework per ICH Q8(R2).** A **Design Space** is established within which CPP variations are acceptable without re-submission. --- **Q4. (b) Discuss the regulatory and clinical challenges related to immunogenicity assessment of biosimilars. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Why Immunogenicity is Critical (1 mark):** Immunogenicity can lead to **loss of efficacy (via neutralising antibodies)**, **altered PK**, and **serious safety events (anaphylaxis, infusion reactions, cross-reactivity with endogenous proteins).** Even minor structural differences between biosimilar and reference can elicit divergent immune responses. **(ii) Regulatory Requirements (2 marks):** - **ICH S6 (Preclinical Safety of Biotechnology-Derived Pharmaceuticals)**, **S8 (Immunotoxicity Studies)**, and **E2A, E2D** for safety reporting. - **EMA Guideline on Immunogenicity Assessment of Biotechnology-Derived Proteins (CHMP/BMWP/14327/2006).** - **US-FDA Immunogenicity Assessment for Therapeutic Protein Products (2014).** - WHO Annex 4 on Similar Biotherapeutic Products. **(iii) Clinical Challenges (2 marks):** - **Two-tier ADA assay design** — screening + confirmatory + titration + nAb. - **Sample timing** — pre-dose, peak, trough, and follow-up (≥ 6 months). - **Assay sensitivity and drug tolerance** — circulating drug can mask ADA detection. - **Patient heterogeneity** — immunogenicity rates vary by indication, concomitant immunosuppression, and HLA genotype. - **Single-arm vs comparative design** — most biosimilar trials compare ADA incidence statistically. **(iv) Interchangeability and Switching (1 mark):** - US-FDA "interchangeable" designation requires **dedicated switching study** to prove no increased immunogenicity or efficacy loss upon alternating. - The **NOR-SWITCH trial** (Lancet, 2017) and **DANBIO registry** are landmark European data supporting CT-P13 (infliximab biosimilar) switching safety. **(v) Risk Management (1 mark):** - **RMP** must include immunogenicity surveillance, **PASS** commitments, and **patient education** materials. - **Distinct INN suffix** (e.g., adalimumab-atto) supports traceability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Conflating immunogenicity with adverse event reporting.** Immunogenicity has dedicated assay and statistical requirements — it is *not* just "side effects." > 2. **Omitting the two-tier assay architecture** — many students state "ADA is measured" but fail to mention screening, confirmatory, and nAb tiers. Expect a **1–2 mark deduction** for this omission. > 3. **Forgetting that biosimilar immunogenicity must be compared *statistically* to the reference product**, not just reported as raw incidence. > 4. **Confusing interchangeability (US-FDA concept) with automatic substitution** (national pharmacy law concept) — these are distinct. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Biosimilars)** ### **Core Definitions** - [ ] **Biosimilar** = biological product *highly similar* to a licensed reference, with *no clinically meaningful differences.* - [ ] **Reference Biologic (RB)** = original innovator product used as the comparison benchmark. - [ ] **Comparability (ICH Q5E)** ≠ Biosimilarity — same principle, different legal context. - [ ] **Interchangeability (US-FDA only)** = additional designation permitting pharmacy-level substitution; requires switching study. ### **Regulatory Authorities** - [ ] **EMA** = 2006 first guidelines; term originator. - [ ] **US-FDA / BPCIA (2010)** = § 351(k) pathway. - [ ] **WHO** = "Similar Biotherapeutic Product (SBP)" term. - [ ] **India / CDSCO + DBT** = 2012/2016 guidelines, RCGM + CDSCO joint review. ### **Analytical Similarity (Cornerstone)** - [ ] **Tier 1** = Critical QAs (potency, key PTMs); strictest statistical criteria. - [ ] **Tier 2** = Key QAs (HMW, certain glycans); wider tolerance. - [ ] **Tier 3** = Other QAs; pharmacopoeial compliance. ### **Totality of Evidence** - [ ] Analytical → Non-clinical → Clinical → Approval. - [ ] Each domain is **necessary but not sufficient**; convergence is mandatory. ### **Key Clinical Concepts** - [ ] **PK Bioequivalence margin** = 90 % CI of geometric mean ratio within **80–125 %**. - [ ] **Equivalence trial** (not superiority) for Phase 3. - [ ] **Extrapolation** = approval across indications based on MoA/PK similarity justification. ### **Immunogenicity** - [ ] Two-tier ADA assay: screening → confirmatory → titration → nAb. - [ ] Risk factors: aggregates, non-human glycans, SC route, patient factors. - [ ] Comparative design — ADA incidence compared statistically. ### **Advanced / Future Trends (High-Yield for 14-mark questions)** - [ ] **AI / Machine Learning** in analytical fingerprinting, glycan prediction, batch comparison. - [ ] **Multi-Attribute Methods (MAM)** — LC-MS-based simultaneous multi-QA monitoring, replacing multiple orthogonal assays. - [ ] **Continuous manufacturing** for biologics — closed-loop bioreactor systems. - [ ] **Patient-centric biosimilars** — auto-injector, pre-filled syringe, biosimilar combinations. - [ ] **Global harmonisation** of biosimilar regulations (WHO-PQ, ICH M11). - [ ] **Real-world evidence (RWE)** in post-marketing biosimilar switching. ### **Economics & Market Access** - [ ] Biosimilar discount typically **15–35 %** (vs. generics at 80–90 %). - [ ] Largest cost-savings therapeutic areas: oncology (rituximab, trastuzumab, bevacizumab), anti-TNF (infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab), insulin, G-CSF. - [ ] **Physician acceptance, hospital tendering, nocebo effect** are the largest market-access barriers. ### **Naming Convention (WHO INN BQ)** - [ ] Biosimilar gets a **4-letter Biological Qualifier suffix**, e.g., *infliximab-dyyb, adalimumab-atto, trastuzumab-dkst*. - [ ] Critical for **pharmacovigilance traceability**. ### **Common "Examiner Traps" — Final Word of Caution** > [!WARNING] > 1. Do **NOT** equate biosimilar = generic. > 2. Do **NOT** claim biosimilar = identical to reference (it is *highly similar*, not identical). > 3. Do **NOT** skip the analytical tier (Tier 1/2/3) framework in 14-mark answers. > 4. Do **NOT** omit the 80–125 % PK equivalence margin — it is **the most-asked number** in KTU exams. > 5. Do **NOT** forget **extrapolation justification** — it is a high-yield, frequently-tested concept. > 6. Always cite **ICH Q5E, Q8, Q9, Q10** wherever relevant — KTU examiners reward guideline-aware answers. --- **End of Module 4 — Topic: Biosimilars — Advances and Challenges** **Course: OEBBT612 — Quality Control in Pharmaceutical Industry | KTU 2024 Scheme**
